The **principal areas of Wales**, comprising the **counties and** **county boroughs of Wales**, are a [form of subdivision](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Wales "Subdivisions of Wales") in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). There are currently 22 principal areas in Wales, and they were established in 1996. They are a single-tier form of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales"), each governed by a [principal council](/wiki/Principal_council "Principal council"), with eleven styled as a "county" (*sir*) and the other eleven as a "county borough" (*bwrdeistref sirol*). They replaced the previous two-tier system of [eight counties](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales") and [37 districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Wales "Districts of Wales") that were in place in Wales [from 1974 to 1996](/wiki/History_of_local_government_in_Wales "History of local government in Wales").

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Principal_areas_of_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales"), Wales is divided into 22 sub-divisions collectively called "principal areas" in the 1994 act. They may be styled as either a "county" or a "county borough". Each principal area is overseen by a "[principal council](/wiki/Principal_council "Principal council")", which may also adopt their principal area style, being called a "county council" ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *cyngor sir*) or a "county borough council" (Welsh: *cyngor bwrdeistref sirol*).[[1]](#cite_note-WGPC-1)[[2]](#cite_note-1994_Act-2)

The basic framework of local government and specifically a council's constitution and general powers were set out in the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), which simplified the [existing local governing structure in Wales that existed prior](/wiki/History_of_local_government_in_Wales "History of local government in Wales"). The later [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994") restructured local government, by significantly amending the previous act. The councils of the principal areas are generally supervised by the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government").[[1]](#cite_note-WGPC-1)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The names of the principal areas, in both English and Welsh, are set out in the 1994 amended version of the 1972 act, under Schedule 4. Section 74 of the 1972 act allows principal councils to change their names, if there is a two-third majority support for such in a specially convened meeting. Since their establishment, multiple councils have pursued a name change. Any notice of a name change has to be submitted to the [Welsh Ministers](/wiki/Welsh_Ministers "Welsh Ministers") and the [Local Democracy and Boundary Commission for Wales](/wiki/Local_Democracy_and_Boundary_Commission_for_Wales "Local Democracy and Boundary Commission for Wales"). The 1972 changes were enacted in 1974 by the then [Conservative administration](/wiki/1970_United_Kingdom_general_election "1970 United Kingdom general election").[[1]](#cite_note-WGPC-1)[[2]](#cite_note-1994_Act-2)[[4]](#cite_note-bbc-4)

The principal areas' councils are [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities "Unitary authorities"), and are sub-divided into [communities](/wiki/Community_(Wales) "Community (Wales)") and [electoral wards](/wiki/List_of_electoral_wards_in_Wales "List of electoral wards in Wales").[[5]](#cite_note-ONS-5)

Some of the principal areas have [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") status, a largely historical status that reflects their historical existence as major population centres.[[5]](#cite_note-ONS-5) The eleven county boroughs of Wales are [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent"), [Bridgend](/wiki/Bridgend_County_Borough "Bridgend County Borough"), [Caerphilly](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough"), [Conwy](/wiki/Conwy_County_Borough "Conwy County Borough"), [Merthyr Tydfil](/wiki/Merthyr_Tydfil_County_Borough "Merthyr Tydfil County Borough"), [Neath Port Talbot](/wiki/Neath_Port_Talbot "Neath Port Talbot"), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), [Rhondda Cynon Taf](/wiki/Rhondda_Cynon_Taf "Rhondda Cynon Taf"), [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen"), [Vale of Glamorgan](/wiki/Vale_of_Glamorgan "Vale of Glamorgan") and [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_County_Borough "Wrexham County Borough"). County borough status does not award any different rights compared to the other counties. The 1994 act stated they should not be treated as a "[borough](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom")" as defined by earlier legislation.[[2]](#cite_note-1994_Act-2)

The other eleven have county status, and are styled as "counties".[[2]](#cite_note-1994_Act-2)

The principal areas' boundaries are made up of its electoral wards, and the average number of electoral wards in a principal area is 40.[[5]](#cite_note-ONS-5)

### Name changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Principal_areas_of_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Name changes")]

Five of the principal areas use different names to those given in the [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994"). In each case the council renamed the area immediately, with the changes taking effect on 2 April 1996.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The changes were:

* [Conwy](/wiki/Conwy_County_Borough "Conwy County Borough") from "Aberconwy and Colwyn"
* [Isle of Anglesey](/wiki/Isle_of_Anglesey "Isle of Anglesey") from "Anglesey"
* [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd") from "Caernarfonshire and Merionethshire"
* [Ceredigion](/wiki/Ceredigion "Ceredigion") from "Cardiganshire"

Other simpler changes also took place such as:

* [Neath Port Talbot](/wiki/Neath_Port_Talbot "Neath Port Talbot") from "Neath and Port Talbot"
* [Rhondda Cynon Taf](/wiki/Rhondda_Cynon_Taf "Rhondda Cynon Taf") from "Rhondda, Cynon, Taff" or "Rhondda Cynon Taff"

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Principal_areas_of_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Following the enacting of the [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994"), the pre-existing eight counties of Wales (now largely the ceremonial [preserved counties of Wales](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales")) and its 37 [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Wales "Districts of Wales") in place since 1974 were replaced on 1 April 1996, with 22 unitary authorities, the "principal areas".[[5]](#cite_note-ONS-5)[[7]](#cite_note-ITVReformPA-7)[[2]](#cite_note-1994_Act-2) The 1994 act also created the communities and preserved counties.[[2]](#cite_note-1994_Act-2)

In 2014, plans were announced to reform local government in Wales, reducing the number of principal areas from 22 to a smaller number of unitary authorities, similar to the counties that they replaced in 1996.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-ITVReformPA-7)

During the [COVID-19 pandemic in Wales](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Wales "COVID-19 pandemic in Wales") in 2020, the principal areas were used as a basis for local [lockdowns](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdowns "COVID-19 lockdowns").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
